addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches ŌĆö when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## claude-in-chrome
**IMPORTANT: If the Chrome browser tools are deferred (must be loaded via ToolSearch before use), load them with ToolSearch before calling them, and batch every tool you expect to need into ONE ToolSearch call (the select query accepts a comma-separated list). Do NOT load tools one at a time; each separate ToolSearch call wastes a full round-trip.**

Start a browser task whose tools are not yet loaded with a single call loading the core set:

ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_context_mcp,mcp__claude-in-chrome__navigate,mcp__claude-in-chrome__computer,mcp__claude-in-chrome__read_page,mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_create_mcp"

Add task-specific tools to the same call when the task obviously needs them: read_console_messages / read_network_requests for debugging, form_input for forms, gif_creator for recordings, javascript_tool for page scripting. Only issue a second ToolSearch if the task later needs a tool you did not anticipate.

## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** ŌĆö if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) ŌĆö if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** ŌĆö for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here ŌĆö don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling ŌĆö if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory ŌĆö the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch ŌĆö load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools ŌĆö that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category ŌĆö the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) ŌåÆ tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) ŌåÆ tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** ŌåÆ tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier ŌĆö bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety ŌĆö treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading ŌĆö hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies ŌĆö verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

## devops-mcp
This DevOps MCP intentionally exposes a small always-on MCP tool surface:
health, list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, and invoke_tool.

For every host, Docker, systemd, file, or audit-log task, call tool_search first
to find the right hidden operation. Then call invoke_tool with the exact operation
name and an args object matching the operation description. Do not assume direct
tools such as run_command, docker_ps, read_file, or service_status are listed in
tools/list; they are discoverable operations to keep client context small.

Call list_capabilities to browse by category or safety class. Call
get_capability_details for one operation's full contract, including args and
examples. Call health first when you need server context, visible tools,
registered agents, or the complete operation-name list. Treat write-capable
operations with care: this server has root-level host access, so prefer
inspection commands before state-changing commands.

## recall-ai
Recall.ai lets developers send meeting bots to video calls, capture meeting data, and connect bots to calendar events. This MCP server provides read-only tools scoped to a Recall.ai workspace. A workspace is the Recall.ai account context that bots, calendars, docs defaults, and logs are read from. By default, the MCP uses the workspace selected when the user authorized the connection. If the account has access to multiple workspaces, workspace-scoped tools accept an optional workspace_id so the agent can read from a different workspace for that call. Use get_info to see the current workspace and API version. When the user names a workspace, call list_workspaces, match the name from the returned list, then pass the returned workspace ID to bot, calendar, log, and docs tools. Do not pass a workspace name as workspace_id. Use bot tools to list bots, inspect bot details and lifecycle events, and page through bot logs when debugging join, recording, or meeting behavior. Use list_calendars/get_calendar to inspect connected and disconnected calendar integrations, platform emails, credential status, and calendar connection/sync state. Use calendar event tools to see which bots were scheduled from calendar events. Use log tools for workspace-level API, calendar connection, recording, transcript, webhook, and integration errors when a bot-specific log query is too narrow. Use list_webhook_endpoints and list_webhook_deliveries when debugging dashboard webhook subscriptions, missing delivery attempts, destination responses, or a resource-specific webhook event such as a bot, calendar, recording, transcript, or artifact event. For usage, consumption, or billing-volume questions (hours or minutes consumed, usage by month/day, usage by platform or by customer metadata, plan limits, usage spikes, top-consuming bots, recording or transcription or storage volume), use the usage tools instead of adding up list_bots results: get_usage_summary for aggregates and plan context, list_bot_usage for per-bot rows such as longest sessions oŌĆ” [truncated]

## synology-monitor
This NAS MCP intentionally exposes only a small always-on tool surface:
list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command,
check_disk_space, and restart_nas_api.

For any NAS diagnostic, troubleshooting, recovery, storage, Drive/ShareSync,
backup, package, log, filesystem, snapshot, permission, or admin task, call
tool_search first unless the task is exactly disk-space checking, free-form
read-only shell diagnosis, or restarting nas-api. Most NAS capabilities are
hidden from tools/list by design to keep the AI session context small.

list_capabilities browses by group and safety class without invoking anything.
get_capability_details returns one operation's full contract. tool_search returns
clear operation descriptions plus the exact invoke_tool call shape. After choosing
a capability, call invoke_tool with the exact returned name, target, and args. Use
target "edgesynology1", "edgesynology2", or "both".

Write tools require a preview first. To execute after reviewing the preview,
call invoke_tool again with confirmed: true inside args. Do not invent or expose
bearer tokens.

## trigger
Trigger.dev MCP server to automate your Trigger.dev projects and answer questions about Trigger.dev by searching the docs. 
If you need help setting up Trigger.dev in your project please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup. 
If the user asks for help with adding Trigger.dev to their project, please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup.

## Windows-MCP

Windows MCP server provides tools to interact directly with the Windows desktop,
thus enabling to operate the desktop on the user's behalf.

aiTitle: Check AG-Grid column group collapse functionality

content.text:
Yes ŌĆö this is a built-in AG Grid feature, and it works well for a 30-column grid. I confirmed it against the Angular 35.1.0 docs via the AG Grid MCP server.

## Short answer

You group columns under a common header, and mark some child columns to **only show when the group is expanded**. Each group header then gets a little expand/collapse toggle. This lets you take 30 columns and show, say, only 8ŌĆō10 "always visible" ones by default, with the rest tucked inside collapsed groups that the user can open on demand.

## How it works

Two pieces do the job:

1. **Column groups** ŌĆö you nest your columns under group definitions (a header with `children`).
2. **`columnGroupShow`** on each child column ŌĆö controls when that column appears:
   - not set ŌåÆ always visible (whether the group is open or closed)
   - `'open'` ŌåÆ only visible when the group is expanded
   - `'closed'` ŌåÆ only visible when the group is collapsed

Example (this is the exact pattern from the docs):

```ts
columnDefs = [
  {
    headerName: 'Group A',
    groupId: 'groupA',          // give it an id so open/closed state survives updates
    children: [
      { field: 'athlete' },                          // always shown
      { field: 'age' },                              // always shown
      { field: 'country', columnGroupShow: 'open' }, // only when expanded
    ],
  },
];
```

So a collapsed "Group A" might show 2 summary columns; expanding it reveals the rest.

## Things worth knowing for a 30-column grid

- **Give every group a `groupId`.** Without it, the open/collapsed state is lost whenever the column definitions change (the group snaps back to closed). This is called out explicitly in the docs.
- **You can control groups programmatically** via the grid API:
  - `setColumnGroupOpened(groupId, true/false)` ŌĆö open/close one group
  - `getColumnGroupState()` / `setColumnGroupState()` ŌĆö save and restore which groups are open (handy if you want to remember the user's layout)
  - `resetColumnGroupState()`
- There's also a `columnGroupOpened` event you can listen to if you need to react when a user expands/collapses.

## One clarification

This collapses *groups of columns you've defined* (e.g. "Pricing", "Dimensions", "Supplier info"). It is **column** grouping ŌĆö not to be confused with **row** grouping (the collapsible row hierarchy on the left).

If you tell me roughly what your 30 columns represent, I can sketch how to bucket them into 5ŌĆō6 collapsible groups with sensible "always visible" summary columns for your dflow PLM grid.

content.content:
# CLAUDE.md ŌĆö Claude Code notes for designflow-frontend

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first. It is the canonical guide for repo structure, custom-code boundaries, current architecture, incidents, deployment, and pending work. This file is only for Claude Code-specific operating notes. For cross-repo infrastructure/runtime standards, see [u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md) and update it when Cloud Run, Cloud Build, domains, BFF routing, secrets/env, storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating rules change.

## Memory and Branch

- User memory file: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`
- Commit and push only to `sandbox-albert`
- Before new code, confirm local `sandbox-albert`, fetch GitHub, merge `origin/develop` into local `sandbox-albert`, push that updated `sandbox-albert` to GitHub, then pull GitHub `sandbox-albert` locally before editing.
- Work only on `sandbox-albert`; use `develop` only for sync/PR base. Never commit to, merge into, or open PRs against `main`.
- Commit locally on `sandbox-albert`, push to `origin/sandbox-albert`, then keep the GitHub PR `sandbox-albert` -> `develop` updated, or create it if missing.

## Ignore Guidance

Use `.claudeignore`. It matches `.cursorignore` and excludes generated, vendored, cache, asset, and legacy/demo template paths such as `dist/`, `node_modules/`, `.angular/`, `.cache/`, `.yarn/`, `coverage/`, `src/assets/`, `src/static-data/`, and Vex demo pages.

## Allowed Operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, run `yarn`/`npm` scripts, run `ng`, use `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push origin sandbox-albert`
- Not allowed by default: force-push, rewrite history, edit `node_modules/` or `dist/`
- SSH is not the normal deployment path. Deployment is Cloud Build -> Docker -> Cloud Run after branch push; use `gcloud` only for explicit rollback or incident triage.

## Tests

```powershell
yarn test
yarn test --testPathPatterns="rfq.grid.config"
yarn test:watch
```

Run the relevant tests before committing. Add or update unit tests for every new or changed function/behavior. The Docker build also runs `yarn test`.

## Claude-Specific Rules

- Use the AG Grid MCP docs before touching AG Grid code: framework `angular`, and set the MCP docs to the closest available v36 version; the repo is on AG Grid `36.0.0`.
- AG Grid modules are registered in ONE place: `src/app/helpers/ag-grid/ag-grid-modules.ts` (read its header). It currently registers `AllEnterpriseModule`. If that ever gets trimmed to a hand-picked list, any new Enterprise feature you use MUST be added there or it silently no-ops in prod. Never register AG Grid modules anywhere else.
- The AG Grid Enterprise license is set globally in `src/main.ts` so lazily-loaded grids are always licensed (a per-module-only setup left the Editor grids showing an "Invalid License" watermark). Feature modules still call `setLicenseKey` redundantly; the `main.ts` call is canonical ŌĆö don't remove it.
- Do not use `.toPromise()` in new code; use `firstValueFrom()`.
- Do not add new `window['anything']` globals; use Angular DI.
- Do not add `aggFunc` to RFQ grid columns without reading `AGENTS.md`.
- For `mat-select`, keep options in a synchronous array, do not mix `[formControl]` with `[(value)]`, and never add `(click)="select.open()"`.

## Commit Style

```text
type(scope): short description
```

Use `fix`, `feat`, `refactor`, `docs`, or `chore`. Keep messages specific.

content.text:
Found them all in [rfq.grid.config.ts](designflow-frontend/src/app/helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq.grid.config.ts:145). The columns are already tagged with `headerClass: "group-header-..."` markers, so the grouping intent is baked into the code. Here are the 8 groups ŌĆö they're a clean cross of **{Gen, Lic}** ├Ś **{FOB, POE, Whse, mDDP}**:

| # | Group | Columns | Count |
|---|-------|---------|-------|
| 1 | **Gen FOB** | Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 3 |
| 2 | **Gen POE** | Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 3 |
| 3 | **Gen Whse** | Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 3 |
| 4 | **Gen mDDP** | Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 3 |
| 5 | **Lic FOB** | Royalty %, Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 4 |
| 6 | **Lic POE** | Royalty %, Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 4 |
| 7 | **Lic Whse** | Royalty %, Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 4 |
| 8 | **Lic mDDP** | Royalty %, Sell Price, Margin %, Price for Sales | 4 |

## One thing to flag

Your count is slightly off, and it's worth knowing before we group them:

- The **4 "Gen" groups have only 3 columns each** (no Royalty % ŌĆö that makes sense, since royalties only apply to licensed product).
- The **4 "Lic" groups have 4 columns each** (they add Royalty %).

So it's **28 pricing columns total**, not 32 ŌĆö laid out as (4 ├Ś 3) + (4 ├Ś 4). Everything is accounted for; there just isn't a 4th "Gen" column to group.

## A suggestion for the collapsed view

Since these are collapsible groups, the natural setup is: when a group is **collapsed**, show just the one number a salesperson usually wants, and hide the rest until expanded. For each group I'd suggest **"Price for Sales"** as the always-visible column (it already has the widest role access ŌĆö `sales`, `designer`, `admin`), with Sell Price / Margin % / Royalty % appearing only when the group is opened.

That would collapse your ~28 pricing columns down to **8 visible columns** by default, each expandable on click.

Want me to implement this? It's a real code change to the RFQ grid, so per your repo rules I'd first sync `sandbox-albert`, check the AG Grid v36 docs (the repo is on 36.0.0, not the 35.1.0 I used for the doc lookup), restructure these into `ColGroupDef` groups with `columnGroupShow`, and update the tests. Just confirm and I'll proceed.

<result>Good - so it's defined but only referenced in tests and a comment. Now I have enough. Let me compile my report.

## Summary Report

### 1. **Flatness Assumptions in Column Config & Display Order**

**File: `rfq.grid.config.ts:1-202`**
- Returns a flat array of 86 `ColDef` objects (line 75 returns `[addColumn(...), addColumn(...), ...]`)
- Comment at line 5 references `buildRfqColumnStateInConfigOrder` but the function is **never actually imported or called** in production code (only in tests)

**File: `rfq-grid-column-display-order.ts:20-58`**
- `buildRfqColumnStateInConfigOrder()` iterates over a flat `colHeaders` array (line 40: `for (const col of colHeaders)`)
- Matches each column by `colId` from the flat array to build output state in config order (lines 44, 49-55)
- **Problem**: If you wrap columns in `ColGroupDef` with nested `children`, this function **will break** because:
  - Line 40's loop expects top-level columns, not groups
  - Line 44 tries to extract `col.colId` from what would be a group object (which has no `colId`, only `groupId` and `children`)
  - The function will produce empty or malformed state

**File: `rfq.component.ts:1006-1017, 2080-2114`**
- Line 1006: `let colHeaders = [...RFQ_COLUMN_HEADERS_CONFIG.columnDefs]` ŌĆö assumes flat array
- Line 1009: `this.filterColumnsByRole(colHeaders, this.role)` ŌĆö filters flat array by role
- Line 1017: `this.colHeaders = colHeaders` ŌĆö stored as flat
- Line 2086: `this.columnDefs = this.applyAutoFormatters(this.colHeaders)` ŌĆö processes flat array
- Line 2109: `this.applyGridViewStateForViewName()` applies saved column state

---

### 2. **Column State Persistence Flow**

**How it works:**
- **Get state**: `gridApi.getColumnState()` returns `ColumnState[]` (line 494, 512, 649 in rfq.component.ts; line 104, 144 in layout-dropdown.component.ts)
- **Save payload**: Sent to backend as array of objects with `{ colId, hide, width, pinned, flex, sort, sortIndex, order, layout_name, field, grid_id }` (layout-dropdown.component.ts:145-154)
- **Backend endpoint**: `POST /saveGridViewState` or legacy `POST /saveGridLayout` (main.service.ts:477, 192)
- **Restore**: `gridApi.applyColumnState({ state, applyOrder: true })` (rfq.component.ts:539, 571, 1896, etc.)
- **Storage**: Backend stores in DB (exact model unknown from this codebase, but accessed via `getGridViewState`, `getSelectedGridView`)

**Missing from current payload**: 
- Ō£Ś `openColumnGroupIds` (AG Grid v36+ column group expand/collapse state)
- Ō£Ś `columnGroupState` (the full group-level state)
- Ō£Ś `columnGroupState[groupId]` ŌåÆ `{ open: boolean, width?: number }`

**What's persisted now**: Order, visibility, width, pinned status, sort ŌĆö **NOT group open/closed state**.

---

### 3. **Grid State Application Method**

**Current approach**: 
- Uses `gridApi.getColumnState()` (legacy method, still valid in v36) ŌåÆ line 112 in layout-dropdown.component.ts
- Uses `gridApi.applyColumnState({ state, applyOrder: true })` ŌåÆ line 94, 539, etc.
- **Not using** `gridApi.getState()` or `initialState` prop (no grep matches found)

**On init**: 
- `onGridReady()` (line 3956 in rfq.component.ts) sets up grid but does **not** apply any column state
- State is applied **after** row data loads, via `applyGridViewStateForViewName()` (line 2109) called from `getAllItem` subscribe callback (line 2107-2113)

---

### 4. **Existing Tests**

**Test file: `rfq-grid-column-display-order.spec.ts`** (47 lines)
- Tests `buildRfqColumnStateInConfigOrder()` function
- Asserts: order preservation, property merging, hide defaults, colId normalization
- **Problem**: Does NOT test nested groups; assumes flat array structure throughout

**Test file: `rfq.grid.config.spec.ts`** (217 lines)
- Tests `RFQ_COLUMN_HEADERS_CONFIG.columnDefs`
- Asserts: column count (86), unique colIds, field/colId presence, role filtering, numeric comparators, sort icons, specific column behaviors (Price Code pinning, Picture filtering, RFQ Group row grouping, etc.)
- Line 130: Expects exactly 86 columns (8├Śgen quote types + 8├Ślic quote types = 32 pricing cols, plus 54 others)
- **Key test**: Lines 120-126 verify column position (factory_updated sits immediately after step)

---

## What Would Break

1. **`buildRfqColumnStateInConfigOrder()`** ŌĆö if called (it's currently unused!), **would crash** trying to iterate nested groups as flat columns
2. **`rfq.component.ts` line 1009** ŌĆö `filterColumnsByRole()` iterates `colHeaders` assuming flat; would skip groups entirely if they became top-level
3. **`rfq.component.ts` line 2086** ŌĆö `applyAutoFormatters()` likely expects flat array; would fail on group objects
4. **`layout-dropdown.component.ts` line 99** ŌĆö column filtering by `colId` assumes every item in `colHeaders` has a `colId`; groups have `groupId` instead
5. **Tests in `rfq-grid-column-display-order.spec.ts`** ŌĆö all assertions assume flat structure
6. **Test in `rfq.grid.config.spec.ts` line 130** ŌĆö column count check would fail (groups + nested columns ŌēĀ 86 flat)

---

## How to Add Group Expand/Collapse Persistence

**Step 1: Extend saved state payload**
- Modify `layout-dropdown.component.ts:145-154` to also capture `gridApi.getColumnGroupState?.()` (if v36 supports it; else use `getState()`)
- Add `columnGroupState: { [groupId: string]: { open: boolean } }` to the shape sent to `saveGridLayout()`

**Step 2: Update backend storage**
- Add `column_group_state` field to the grid view/layout table (parallel to `column_state`)

**Step 3: Restore on init**
- In `applyGridViewStateForViewName()` (rfq.component.ts:521), after `applyColumnState()`, also call:
  ```ts
  if (res.column_group_state) {
    this.gridApi.applyColumnGroupState(res.column_group_state);
  }
  ```

**Step 4: Handle flatŌåÆgroup migration**
- Keep `buildRfqColumnStateInConfigOrder()` BUT refactor it to walk both flat columns AND nested columns in groups
- Or: Move group definitions into `rfq.grid.config.ts` so role filtering happens at group level, not individual column level

---

## Files to Touch

| File | Change | Rationale |
|------|--------|-----------|
| `rfq.grid.config.ts:75-201` | Refactor `getColumnDefs()` return to nest pricing columns in 8 `ColGroupDef` with `children` | Define groups |
| `rfq-grid-column-display-order.ts:20-58` | Rewrite to recursively walk groups + flat columns | Fix breakage |
| `rfq-grid-column-display-order.spec.ts:1-47` | Add tests for nested group structures | Maintain coverage |
| `rfq.component.ts:1009` | Update `filterColumnsByRole()` to handle groups | Role filtering |
| `rfq.component.ts:2086` | Update `applyAutoFormatters()` to handle nested cols | Formatter application |
| `rfq.component.ts:521-578` | Add `columnGroupState` restoration in `applyGridViewStateForViewName()` | Restore group state |
| `layout-dropdown.component.ts:138-164` | Extend payload to include `column_group_state` in `onSave()` | Save group state |
| `main.service.ts:477-482` | Extend `saveGridViewState`/`getGridViewState` types to include `column_group_state` field | Persist to backend |
| `rfq.grid.config.spec.ts:130` | Update column count assertion and add group structure tests | Test groups |

One important design realization before I present the plan: nesting the config array directly (the agent's default assumption) would break role-filtering, auto-formatters, and the layout dropdown ŌĆö a big, risky refactor. There's a lower-risk approach where grouping happens as a *final transform* after role-filtering, keeping all the flat machinery intact. Let me verify one thing that decides scope: whether saved views are stored as a flexible JSON doc (frontend-only change) or rigid per-column rows (needs multi-repo backend work).

content.text: Saved views are stored as **per-column rows** (`{colId, hide, pinned, width, order, layout_name, field, grid_id}`) ŌĆö one record per column. That schema has no natural slot for group open/closed state (which is `{groupId, open}[]`, not per-column). This is the one decision that changes scope, so let me check how rigid the backend storage is before finalizing.

# CLAUDE.md ŌĆö Claude Code notes for designflow-backend

Read [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) first. It is the canonical guide for repo structure, custom-code boundaries, data model, migrations, deployment, incidents, and pending work. This file is only for Claude Code-specific operating notes. For cross-repo infrastructure/runtime standards, see [u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md) and update it when Cloud Run, Cloud Build, domains, BFF routing, secrets/env, storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating rules change.

## Memory and Branch

- User memory file: `C:\Users\ahazan2\.claude\projects\D--repos-dflow\memory\MEMORY.md`
- Active branch: `sandbox-albert`
- Commit and push only to `sandbox-albert`
- Merge or switch branches only when the user explicitly asks; normal delivery is PR to `develop`

## Ignore Guidance

Use `.claudeignore`. It matches `.cursorignore` and excludes generated, vendored, cache, Playwright, runtime upload, and large data paths such as `node_modules/`, `coverage/`, `dist/`, `.cache/`, `images/`, `thumbs/`, and `data/hts-rates.json`.

## Allowed Operations

- Allowed: read/edit repo files, run `npm` scripts, use `git add`, `git commit`, and `git push origin sandbox-albert`
- Not allowed by default: force-push, rewrite history, edit `node_modules/`
- SSH is not the normal deployment path. Deployment is Cloud Build -> Docker -> Cloud Run after branch push; use `gcloud` only for explicit rollback or incident triage.

## Tests

```powershell
npm test
npm test -- --testPathPattern="<name>"
```

Run the relevant tests before committing. `start:sandbox-albert` does not run tests before starting `nodemon`.

## Claude-Specific Rules

- For DB columns: model field + `models/db.js` inline migration + `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` assertion.
- Quote mixed-case Postgres column identifiers in inline migrations.
- Add code to `routes/lib.router.js` and `models/lib.model.js` surgically; they are large legacy files.
- Keep secrets out of `--set-env-vars`; use Secret Manager via `--set-secrets`.

## Commit Style

```text
type(scope): short description
```

Use `fix`, `feat`, `refactor`, `docs`, or `chore`. Keep messages specific.

content.text:
I now have the full picture. One nice discovery: the backend `GridChildrenLayout` model ([GridChildrenLayout.js:71](designflow-backend/models/db/GridChildrenLayout.js)) already stores `columnGroupShow` and `headerName` per column ŌĆö so the schema is already partway toward column groups. Here's my plan.

---

## Plan: Collapsible column groups for the 8 RFQ pricing groups

### The 8 groups (28 columns, confirmed)
`{Gen, Lic}` ├Ś `{FOB, POE, Whse, mDDP}`. "Gen" groups have 3 columns, "Lic" groups have 4 (they add Royalty %). **"Price for Sales"** stays always-visible in each group; the rest show only when the group is expanded. **Default = expanded** (nothing collapsed until the user chooses).

### Design decision ŌĆö how to add the groups (this is the important one)
The Explore trace showed the RFQ column array is consumed **flat** in several places: `filterColumnsByRole`, `applyAutoFormatters`, and the layout dropdown's `_filter` all iterate expecting each entry to have a `colId`. **Nesting the config array directly into `ColGroupDef` would break all of those** (groups have `groupId`/`children`, no `colId`).

So instead of nesting the source config, I'll **keep the config flat and add one pure transform function** that wraps columns into the 8 `ColGroupDef` groups *as the very last step*, after role-filtering and formatters have run on the flat list. This keeps every existing flat code path and its tests untouched.

- The columns are already tagged with `headerClass: "group-header-gen-fob"` etc. ŌĆö the transform reads that marker (or a small prefix map) to decide grouping, so grouping intent stays declared in one place.
- Each `ColGroupDef` gets a stable `groupId` (`grp_gen_fob`, ŌĆ”) ŌĆö **required** for AG Grid to persist open/closed state ŌĆö plus `openByDefault: true` (starts expanded), `marryChildren: true` (keeps a group's columns together).
- Children get `columnGroupShow: 'open'` **except** "Price for Sales" (no `columnGroupShow` ŌåÆ always visible).
- `colHeaders` stays flat for state purposes; only the final `columnDefs` handed to `<ag-grid-angular>` is nested.

### Persistence (per AG Grid v36 docs I pulled)
Column group open/closed state is **separate** from column state: `api.getColumnGroupState()` ŌåÆ `{groupId, open}[]`, restored with `api.setColumnGroupState(...)`. The existing saved-view pipeline only stores per-column rows (`hide/width/pinned/order`), so the group open/closed bit needs a home. That's the one scope fork ŌĆö see the question below.

### Files (frontend, all under `designflow-frontend`)
| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq-column-groups.ts` *(new)* | Pure `groupRfqPricingColumns(flatCols)` transform + groupId map |
| `helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq-column-groups.spec.ts` *(new)* | Unit tests: correct 8 groups, right children, Price-for-Sales always-visible, non-pricing columns pass through untouched, order preserved |
| `pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts` | Apply transform where `this.columnDefs` is built (~line 2086); restore group state in `applyGridViewStateForViewName` |
| `pages/rfq/layout-dropdown/layout-dropdown.component.ts` | Capture `getColumnGroupState()` in `onSave` |
| `helpers/ag-grid/ag-grid-modules.ts` | Verify `ColumnApiModule`/grouping is registered (already `AllEnterpriseModule`, so fine) |

`rfq.grid.config.ts` stays flat ŌåÆ its 86-column spec stays green. The dead `buildRfqColumnStateInConfigOrder` helper is left alone.

### Process (your rules)
0. **Git sync first**: on GitHub, update `sandbox-albert` from `develop`; pull GitHub `sandbox-albert` into local; then code. Only ever touch `sandbox-albert` (and `develop` as base) ŌĆö never `main`.
1. Read the relevant `.md` files first (`AGENTS.md`, `docs/ag-grid-36-migration-plan.md`, `pages/rfq/README.md`, `docs/architecture.md`).
2. All AG Grid work verified against the AG-Grid MCP `search_docs` (Angular, v35.1.0 ŌĆö closest the MCP exposes to the repo's v36).
3. Unit tests for every new/changed function; run `yarn test --testPathPatterns="rfq"` before commit.

### The one open decision
How the user's collapse choice should persist ("save the view on their own"):

questions.question: How should each user's collapse/expand choice be saved?

options.label: Server-side saved view (recommended)
options.description: Extend the existing per-user saved-view feature end-to-end so the collapse state travels with their named view like column order/width already do. Requires a small backend change: one field on GridChildrenLayout/GridLayout + inline migration + migration test in designflow-backend (documented pattern), and passing it through the BFF. Matches 'save the view on their own' exactly and syncs across devices.

options.label: Frontend-only (localStorage)
options.description: Save the group open/closed state in the browser per user+view via localStorage. No backend/BFF changes, fastest and lowest-risk, stays entirely in designflow-frontend. Downside: collapse state is per-browser ŌĆö it won't follow the user to another device and isn't tied to the server-side view record.

options.label: Session-only, no save
options.description: Groups just start expanded every load (openByDefault); collapsing works during the session but isn't remembered. Simplest, but doesn't meet the 'save the view' requirement.

content.text:
Locked in: **server-side saved view**. Here's the finalized plan. This makes it a multi-repo change (frontend + backend + BFF pass-through), so I've noted that explicitly ŌĆö and per your memory (the Codex concurrency incident), I'll work the repos **sequentially**, never two agents on the backend tree at once.

## Finalized plan

### Frontend ŌĆö `designflow-frontend`
1. **New `helpers/ag-grid/rfq-grid/rfq-column-groups.ts`** ŌĆö pure `groupRfqPricingColumns(flatCols)` that wraps the 28 pricing columns into 8 `ColGroupDef`s (`groupId` = `grp_gen_fob`ŌĆ”`grp_lic_mddp`, `openByDefault: true`, `marryChildren: true`), sets `columnGroupShow:'open'` on all children except "Price for Sales", and passes non-pricing columns through untouched. Runs **after** role-filter/formatters, so nothing flat breaks.
2. **`pages/rfq/rfq.component.ts`** ŌĆö call the transform where `this.columnDefs` is assembled (~2086); in `applyGridViewStateForViewName` (~521), after `applyColumnState(...)`, call `setColumnGroupState(savedGroupState)`.
3. **`pages/rfq/layout-dropdown/layout-dropdown.component.ts`** ŌĆö in `onSave`, also capture `gridApi.getColumnGroupState()` and include it in the `saveGridLayout` payload.
4. **`main.service.ts`** ŌĆö widen the save/get types to carry the group state.

### Backend ŌĆö `designflow-backend`
5. Persist group open/closed on the **per-view** record. Cleanest fit: a `col_group_state` text/JSON field on **`GridLayout`** (one row per view) holding the `{groupId, open}[]` array ŌĆö rather than scattering it across per-column `GridChildrenLayout` rows. Per your backend rules: model field + `models/db.js` inline migration (quoted mixed-case identifier) + assertion in `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js`.
6. Update `grid.service.js` / `main.controller.js` save+fetch handlers to read/write the new field.

### BFF ŌĆö `designflow-bff`

- **Step 0 ŌĆō git sync**: on GitHub, merge `develop` ŌåÆ `sandbox-albert`; pull GitHub `sandbox-albert` into each local repo; only then edit. Touch only `sandbox-albert` (+`develop` as base) ŌĆö never `main`, in any repo.

message.content: we already have a Save View function, you're using that one, right? and that one's server-side?

content.text:
Yes ŌĆö I'm extending the one you already have, not building a new one. To confirm what I actually traced in the code:

**It's the existing "Save View" (layout dropdown), and it is server-side:**

- The UI is [layout-dropdown.component.ts](designflow-frontend/src/app/pages/rfq/layout-dropdown/layout-dropdown.component.ts) ŌĆö its `onSave()` grabs the current column state and calls `itemService.saveGridLayout(...)`.
- That call goes over the network (through the BFF) to `designflow-backend`, which writes it to the database ŌĆö the `GridLayout` / `GridChildrenLayout` tables ([GridChildrenLayout.js](designflow-backend/models/db/GridChildrenLayout.js)), keyed per user and per view name. So a user's saved view already follows them across devices/sessions.

Today that saved record stores column **order, width, pinned, hide** ŌĆö but **not** whether a column group is collapsed (AG Grid keeps group open/closed state in a separate structure). So the only new thing is: capture that one extra bit (`getColumnGroupState()`) in the same `onSave()`, carry it through the same endpoint, and store it on the same per-view record ŌĆö then re-apply it when the view loads.

Nothing new gets created; the collapse choice just rides along inside the Save View you already use. That's exactly why the plan touches the backend (one small field on the existing `GridLayout` record) ŌĆö so it persists server-side like the rest of the view, instead of only in the browser.

content.text: The restore reads from a newer **view-state** store (`getGridViewState` ŌåÆ `res.column_state` + `res.filter_model`), while the layout dropdown's `onSave` uses the legacy `saveGridLayout`. I need to find where the newer view-state is *saved* so I add `column_group_state` on the correct path.
The RFQ save path is `rfq.component.ts:497`. Let me read that save function to add `column_group_state` there.

192	    // One-time backfill: set factory_country from the most common vendor_country
193	    // among vendors linked to each factory. Only fills NULLs so it is safe to re-run.
194	    sequelize.query(`
195	      UPDATE ${schema}"Factory" f
196	      SET factory_country = sub.country
197	      FROM (
198	        SELECT factory_id_fk, vendor_country AS country,
199	               ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY factory_id_fk ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rn
200	        FROM ${schema}vendor
201	        WHERE factory_id_fk IS NOT NULL AND vendor_country IS NOT NULL AND vendor_country <> ''

feat(rfq): collapsible pricing column groups with per-view persistence

Wrap the 28 RFQ pricing columns into 8 collapsible AG Grid column groups
(Gen/Lic x FOB/POE/Whse/mDDP). "Price for Sales" stays always-visible; the
other columns show only when a group is expanded. Groups start expanded.

Grouping is applied as the final step when building the grid columnDefs, after
role filtering and formatters run on the flat list, so existing flat-array code
paths are untouched. Each user's collapse choice persists via the existing Save
View feature: getColumnGroupState() is captured on save and setColumnGroupState()
re-applied on load (stored server-side alongside column_state).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Both repos already have standing `sandbox-albert ŌåÆ develop` PRs ([frontend #141](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/141), [backend #58](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-backend/pull/58)) ŌĆö my pushes updated them automatically. Done.

## Summary

Collapsible column groups for the 8 RFQ pricing groups are implemented, tested, committed, and pushed.

**What it does:** the 28 pricing columns are wrapped into 8 collapsible AG Grid groups ŌĆö `{Gen, Lic}` ├Ś `{FOB, POE, Whse, mDDP}`. "Price for Sales" stays visible when a group is collapsed; Sell Price / Margin % / Royalty % show only when expanded. Groups start **expanded** (nothing collapsed by default). Each user's collapse choices ride along in the **existing Save View** feature, stored server-side.

**Frontend** ([designflow-frontend](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/141), commit `e8337174`)
- `rfq-column-groups.ts` ŌĆö new pure transform that builds the 8 `ColGroupDef`s from the `headerClass` markers already on the columns. Runs *after* role-filtering/formatters, so no existing flat-array code path changed.
- `rfq.component.ts` ŌĆö applies the transform to `columnDefs`; captures `getColumnGroupState()` on save and re-applies via `setColumnGroupState()` on view load.
- `main.service.ts` ŌĆö `saveGridViewState` now carries `column_group_state`.
- `rfq-column-groups.spec.ts` ŌĆö 12 tests.

**Backend** ([designflow-backend](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-backend/pull/58), commit `fa6aad1`)
- New `GridViewState.column_group_state` JSONB column + startup `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` migration (auto-runs on deploy ŌĆö no manual DB step).
- `saveGridViewState`/`getGridViewState` read/write/return it.
- Migration + model test assertions added.

**BFF:** no change ŌĆö it's a transparent proxy for these endpoints.

**Tests:** all green ŌĆö backend 89/89, transform 12/12, rfq config/display-order 24/24, rfq.component 55/55, all against AG Grid docs (Angular 35.1.0) per your rule.

**Process:** synced all three repos with `develop` first (stash ŌåÆ merge ŌåÆ restore); your in-progress work was preserved. On the backend, `lib.model.js` also held your uncommitted quote-date WIP (`resolveQuoteDateForPriceUpdate`/`normalizePrice`) ŌĆö I staged **only my hunks** via a filtered patch, so that WIP remains uncommitted in your working tree. Nothing touched `main`.

**One thing to confirm:** the RFQ page calls `saveGridViewState` in `onSaveNewView` (creating/overwriting a named view). So a user's collapse state persists when they **save a view by name**. If your intended UX is a separate "update current view" button that should also persist collapse changes, tell me and I'll wire that trigger too.

Want me to run the app and show the groups collapsing, or is the code delivery enough for now?

content.text:
Now let me answer your question in plain terms.

**What "saveGridViewState" is:** it's just the internal name of the function that saves a grid View to the server. When you arrange the RFQ grid the way you like and hit a **Save** button, that function bundles up your layout (column order, widths, what's hidden, filters) and stores it under your view's name so it comes back next time. My job was to add one more thing to that bundle: *which pricing groups you've collapsed*.

**Why I flagged it:** your app actually has **two** Save buttons, and they call that function in two different places:
1. **"Save" a brand-new view** (typing a name and saving it) ŌĆö I had wired this one.
2. **"Save" changes to the view you're already on** (the "Current view was saved successfully" button) ŌĆö I had **missed** this one.

So as I'd left it, collapsing groups would have been remembered only when you *created* a view, not when you *updated* an existing one. That's the doubt I was raising ŌĆö and checking just now confirmed the gap was real. **I've just fixed it**, so collapse state now saves from both buttons. Let me verify and re-push.

content.text: You're right that there's a real smell here ŌĆö and it's worth being precise about what should and shouldn't be merged. Let me look at both functions in full before I answer.

Here's my honest take: they should **not** be literally the same function ŌĆö each does genuinely different surrounding work (creating a view adds it to your list and shows "created"; saving the current one guards the Default view and shows "saved"). Collapsing those into one method would make it messier, not cleaner.

But you've put your finger on the actual flaw: the **payload** ŌĆö the part that decides *what a saved view contains* ŌĆö was copy-pasted into both. That duplication is exactly why I was able to add the group state to one and forget the other. The right fix is to pull that shared core into one small helper that both call, so "what's in a saved view" lives in a single place. Let me do that.

content.text:
Fixed and pushed to [PR #141](https://github.com/popcre/designflow-frontend/pull/141).

**You were right to push on it.** Here's what changed:

- **Before:** the two Save buttons each had their own copy of the "what goes into a saved view" code. Same list, written twice. That's what let me update one and miss the other.
- **After:** there's now **one** small shared helper (`saveRfqViewState`) that builds that list. Both buttons call it. So "what a saved view contains" is defined in exactly one place ŌĆö if anyone adds something new to a saved view later, it automatically applies to both Save buttons and can't be forgotten on one.

I kept the two buttons as separate functions (they still do different things around the save ŌĆö one adds the view to your list and says "created," the other guards the Default view and says "saved"), but the duplicated part is now gone.

I also added **3 tests** that specifically check both Save buttons persist the collapse state, plus one that confirms you still can't overwrite the Default view. So this exact bug can't come back silently. All 58 component tests pass.

The judgment you applied is a good one to keep applying: when the same logic is written in two places, one will eventually drift from the other.

Understood ŌĆö that means committing the remaining **pre-existing WIP** (the quote-date logic, e2e tests, etc.) that I'd been deliberately leaving untouched, and pushing everything. Since this is work I didn't author, let me first see exactly what's outstanding in each repo so I commit it accurately.

+### Item Detail Main-tab layout, image-upload, and Tech Pack files (2026-06-29 ŌåÆ 2026-06-30)
 
-**Looks like:** The Item Detail page (`pages/itemDetail/itemDetail.component.{html,scss}`, class `.df-redesign`) just drops an `<image-upload>` into a left column.
+**Looks like:** The Item Detail page (`pages/itemDetail/itemDetail.component.{html,scss,ts}`, class `.df-redesign`) just drops an `<image-upload>` into a left column with some cards.
 
-**Actually, three things bite:**
+**Quirks that bite:**
 
 1. **There are TWO image-upload components.** Item Detail uses `<image-upload>` (selector `image-upload`, `pages/itemDetail/image-upload/`, declared in `itemDetail.module.ts`). `components/image-upload/` is a *different* component (selector `app-image-upload`) ŌĆö do not edit it expecting Item Detail to change.
-2. **The `image-upload` template was built for a wide, full-width strip.** Its internal `.bottom-strip` is a flex **row** (`file-upload` drop box on the left, the `p-carousel` thumbnail strip on the right). The page wraps it in `.df-grid-main` whose left column is `minmax(0, 2fr)` (~40% of the page) and right column `3fr` (~60%, the Attributes card). Earlier the left column was a fixed `320px`, which squished the image and pushed the carousel into dead space. If you change the column ratio, re-check the `image-upload.component.scss` `.file-upload`/`.thumbnail-strip` widths.
-3. **The drag-to-upload target must be explicitly sized.** The droppable `<div vexFileDragNDrop>` originally only wrapped the icon+label, so there was almost nothing to drop onto. It now carries class `.dropzone-area` (`min-height:150px`, full width) in the template + scss. The `vexFileDragNDrop` directive (standalone, imported from `pages/rfq/upload-file/file-drag-n-drop.directive.ts`) paints its own dashed border/bg via `@HostBinding` and emits `filesChangeEmitter`; the template *also* binds `(drop)`/`(dragover)` ŌĆö both fire, which is harmless.
+2. **The `image-upload` template was built for a wide, full-width strip.** Its internal `.bottom-strip` is a flex **row** (`file-upload` drop box left, `p-carousel` thumbnail strip right). The page wraps it in `.df-grid-main` = `minmax(0, 2fr) minmax(0, 1fr)` (left ~40% image-only, right ~60%). If you change the ratio, re-check `image-upload.component.scss` `.file-upload`/`.thumbnail-strip` widths.
+3. **Current layout (post 06-30):** LEFT column = image gallery + upload/carousel only. RIGHT column (`.df-main-right`, a flex column) = Item Attributes, then `.df-right-pair` (Product Team | Comments, 2-col), then `.df-grid-bottom--pair` (narrow Tech Pack & Files | Tags). There is **no full-width bottom row** anymore ŌĆö the secondary cards live in the right column to fill it and balance the tall left column. Product Team members render in a 2-col `.df-team-grid`.
+4. **The image drop-target must be explicitly sized.** `<div class="dropzone-area" vexFileDragNDrop>` (`min-height:150px`, full width) ŌĆö originally it only wrapped the icon+label so there was nothing to drop onto. The `vexFileDragNDrop` directive (standalone, from `pages/rfq/upload-file/file-drag-n-drop.directive.ts`) paints its own dashed border/bg via `@HostBinding` and emits `filesChangeEmitter`; the template *also* binds `(drop)`/`(dragover)` ŌĆö both fire, harmless.
+5. **Carousel:** `p-carousel` is `[numVisible]="3" [numScroll]="1"` with `[showIndicators]` dots; the active dot is styled larger via `.p-carousel-indicator.p-highlight button` in `image-upload.component.scss`. PrimeNG-rendered ŌĆö verify dot count/active-tracking in the live app.
+6. **Tech-pack files share the gallery's `itemAttachments` array.** Gallery images use the capitalized display names `Image`/`Thumb` (image-upload `saveImageLink`); tech-pack/document uploads use the **lowercased MIME prefix** `contentType.split('/')[0]` (e.g. `application`, `image`) ŌĆö see `components/file-upload/file-upload-dialog.component.ts` and the new `uploadTechpackFiles` in `itemDetail.component.ts`. So to list tech-pack files, **case-sensitively exclude `Image`/`Thumb`** (that is exactly what `refreshTechpackFiles()` does). The old "View Uploaded Files" dialog showed ALL attachments unfiltered. The Tech Pack card now uploads directly (HelperService `saveToDigitalOceanAndDB` + `itemService.saveTechLink`, mirroring file-upload-dialog) and shows the filtered list inline with type badges.
+7. **Active status is single-source.** The top-bar Active pill duplicated `item_active_status` (same source as the Attributes "Active Status" field, derived at `getItemInfo` via `yesNoOptions.find(... item_active_status)`). The pill was removed; Active Status now lives only in the **Second** attribute tab (read-only + edit mode).
 
-**Why:** Owner (non-dev) asked for a 40% image column, the alternate-image carousel under the image, no dead right-side space, and a working drop target. Layout-only change (HTML + SCSS), committed `bda0dc30`, PR #140 (`sandbox-albert ŌåÆ develop`).
+**Why:** Owner (non-dev) iteratively asked for a 40% image column, carousel under the image, no dead right-side space, narrower Product Team, a right-column Tech Pack card with drag-drop + inline file names, and de-duplicated Active status. Layout + `image-upload` + a new tech-pack upload/list path. Commits `bda0dc30` (initial) and `15ecd7e5` (06-30), PR #140 (`sandbox-albert ŌåÆ develop`).
 
-**Future sessions should:** Verify layout changes with the static-CSS harness approach if the local backend stack (BFF + 4 services on :5000ŌĆō5003 + Azure SSO) isn't up ŌĆö compile the two SCSS files with `npx sass`, strip `::ng-deep`, render the real DOM structure with sample data. Load the Material Icons webfont in any such harness or `<mat-icon>` ligatures render as raw words (e.g. `grid_view`) and look like overlapping text ŌĆö that is a harness artifact, not an app bug. The drag-drop *upload round-trip* (to DigitalOcean/DB) was NOT verified end-to-end; only the drop-target sizing was.

file.content:
name: Forbid Shared DB Bypass

on:
  pull_request:
  push:
    branches: [develop]

jobs:
  forbid-shared-db-bypass:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Reject app-owned shared database changes
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail

          if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" = "pull_request" ]; then
            base="${{ github.event.pull_request.base.sha }}"
          elif [ -n "${{ github.event.before }}" ] && [ "${{ github.event.before }}" != "0000000000000000000000000000000000000000" ]; then
            base="${{ github.event.before }}"
          else
            base="$(git rev-list --max-parents=0 HEAD)"
          fi

          changed="$(git diff --name-only "$base" HEAD)"
          printf '%s\n' "$changed"

          if printf '%s\n' "$changed" | grep -E '^supabase/migrations/.*\.sql$'; then
            echo "Shared Supabase migrations must be authored in u2giants/shared-db, not this app repo."
            exit 1
          fi

          scan_files="$(printf '%s\n' "$changed" | grep -E '^(\.github/workflows/|scripts/|package\.json|package-lock\.json)' | grep -v '^\.github/workflows/forbid-shared-db-bypass\.yml$' || true)"
          if [ -n "$scan_files" ]; then
            pattern='supabase[[:space:]]+db[[:space:]]+(push|reset)|supabase[[:space:]]+migration[[:space:]]+repair|schema_migrations|SUPABASE_DB_PASSWORD|SUPABASE_DB_URL|POSTGRES_URL_NON_POOLING'
            if printf '%s\n' "$scan_files" | xargs grep -nE "$pattern"; then
              echo "App repos may not mutate or repair the shared Supabase database. Put DB changes in u2giants/shared-db."
              exit 1
            fi
          fi

content.text: My change added an **inline `ALTER TABLE` in `db.js`**, not a `supabase/migrations/*.sql` file ŌĆö so it slips past this regex. But the *policy* is unambiguous: "App repos may not mutate the shared Supabase database. Put DB changes in `u2giants/shared-db`." A runtime `ALTER TABLE` on the shared DB is exactly what this forbids in spirit. Let me read the canonical process in the `.md` files.

content.content:
5:> **Supabase migration:** Cloud SQL ŌåÆ Supabase work is governed by the workspace-wide [`SUPABASE-MIGRATION.md`](SUPABASE-MIGRATION.md), the canonical copy of which lives in this repo. Read it before changing DB config, pooling, schema, or data-access patterns. This is the **first** backend service tested against Supabase; keep Sequelize and existing auth/business/file behavior intact.
42:| DB schema / migration / new column / data flow | `AGENTS.md` (┬¦6 Decision tree, ┬¦8 Data model), `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/configuration.md` if env affected | `docs/deployment.md` unless rollout changes |
87:| `models/db.js` | Sequelize init + model registration + **all schema migrations** (`ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS`) |
123:- `models/db/*.js` model files are effectively schema declarations. When you add a column to a model, you **must** also add the matching `ALTER TABLE ŌĆ” ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` migration in `models/db.js` or the column will never exist in production. See ┬¦6 Decision Tree.
149:2. Add `sequelize.query(\`ALTER TABLE ${schema}"TableName" ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "col_name" TYPE\`).catch(...)` inside `initialize()` in `models/db.js`. **If the column name is mixed-case (e.g. `rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots`), it MUST be double-quoted in the ALTER TABLE.** Sequelize quotes all identifiers, so an unquoted mixed-case name is folded to lowercase by Postgres and Sequelize never finds it ŌĆö `findAll` then throws and the grid breaks (see ┬¦14, SYS-002).
151:4. Add a regression assertion in `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js`
157:3. Write a `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` migration in `models/db.js`
204:| Add/change a DB column | `models/db/<Model>.js`, `models/db.js` (migration), `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` | Any other file unless the column needs a new service method |
224:| `users` | `user_id`, `email`, `name`, `role`, `profile_photo` | `models/db/users.js` | `profile_photo` was added via safe migration (SYS-030 ŌĆö see ┬¦14). Auto-provisioned on first Azure SSO login with `level: 'designer'`. |
227:| `Factory` | `factory_id`, `factory_nickname`, `factory_country` | `models/db/Factory.js` | `factory_country` added May 2026 via safe migration; backfilled from most common `vendor_country` |
228:| `RFQVendor` | `RFQVendor_id`, `RFQitem_id_fk`, `vendor_id_fk`, `RFQVendor_amount`, `quote_date`, `req_status`, `requote_requested` | `models/db/RFQVendor.js` | One factory's quote on one RFQ item. `RFQVendor_id` is the `quote_id` the frontend sends. **`vendor_id_fk` stores `Factory.id`, not `Vendor.vendor_id`** (see ┬¦11). `requote_requested` (BOOLEAN, added 2026-06 via safe migration) flags a stale price for refresh; mapped in `helpers/utility.js` `remapRFQItem`. |
231:| `ai_cache_events` | `provider`, `api_backend`, `model`, `cache_strategy`, token/cache counters, `feature`, `session_id` | `models/db/AiCacheEvent.js` | Append-only AI cache telemetry. `services/ai-cache-telemetry.service.js` aggregates this for Admin ŌåÆ AI Settings. Created by startup migration in `models/db.js`. |
309:**Do not change because:** Splitting would touch every import and require a coordinated, carefully tested migration. The API surface is still in flux.
323:### Migrations live in `models/db.js`, not a migrations folder
325:**Looks like:** The wrong place for schema changes.
331:// Inside initialize() in models/db.js, after sequelize.sync():
332:sequelize.query(`ALTER TABLE ${schema}"TableName" ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "col_name" TYPE`)
336:`${schema}` resolves to `"designflow".` in prod (or empty in test envs). Always add a matching assertion to `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js`.
338:[Omitted long matching line]
486:### 2026-05-29 ŌĆö SYS-002: RFQ grid empty after unquoted `price_sales_snapshots` migration
490:[Omitted long matching line]
492:**Recovery:** Quoted the identifier (commit `a556d3f` ŌåÆ `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "rfqItem_price_sales_snapshots" TEXT`) and added `ALTER TABLE "RFQItem" DROP COLUMN IF EXISTS "rfqitem_price_sales_snapshots"` (commit `476e453`) to remove the empty lowercase orphan column. Corrected `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` to require the quoted form and added guards: fail on the unquoted form, assert the orphan DROP exists, assert the correct mixed-case column is never dropped.
494:**Rule:** Inline `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` migrations in `models/db.js` **must quote any mixed-case identifier**. Sequelize quotes identifiers; Postgres folds unquoted ones to lowercase, creating an orphan column Sequelize can never find. See ┬¦6 and ┬¦11.
500:**What happened:** `profile_photo` was added to the `users` Sequelize model (`models/db/users.js`) in commit `53cc531` without adding the matching `ALTER TABLE` migration in `models/db.js`. Every `sql.users.findOne()` in production failed with `column "profile_photo" does not exist`, crashing login.
502:**Root cause:** Sequelize `.sync()` does not alter existing tables. Adding a field to a model without a corresponding migration is a silent no-op in dev (if the dev DB already has the column) but fails in prod.
504:**Recovery:** Added the `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` migration to `models/db.js` and deployed.
506:**Rule:** Whenever you add a field to a Sequelize model in `models/db/*.js`, you **must** also add `ALTER TABLE ŌĆ” ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` in `models/db.js`. Add an assertion to `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js`.
554:**Prevention (in place):** `client_encoding: 'UTF8'` added to `dialectOptions` in both `designflow-backend/models/db.js` and `designflow-data-syncing/models/db.js`. This forces UTF-8 on the connection so the app can't introduce new mojibake; it does **not** retro-fix existing data.

Corrected `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` to require the quoted form and added guards: fail on the unquoted form, assert the orphan DROP exists, assert the correct mixed-case column is never dropped.
494:**Rule:** Inline `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` migrations in `models/db.js` **must quote any mixed-case identifier**. Sequelize quotes identifiers; Postgres folds unquoted ones to lowercase, creating an orphan column Sequelize can never find. See ┬¦6 and ┬¦11.
500:**What happened:** `profile_photo` was added to the `users` Sequelize model (`models/db/users.js`) in commit `53cc531` without adding the matching `ALTER TABLE` migration in `models/db.js`. Every `sql.users.findOne()` in production failed with `column "profile_photo" does not exist`, crashing login.
502:**Root cause:** Sequelize `.sync()` does not alter existing tables. Adding a field to a model without a corresponding migration is a silent no-op in dev (if the dev DB already has the column) but fails in prod.
504:**Recovery:** Added the `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` migration to `models/db.js` and deployed.
506:**Rule:** Whenever you add a field to a Sequelize model in `models/db/*.js`, you **must** also add `ALTER TABLE ŌĆ” ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` in `models/db.js`. Add an assertion to `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js`.
554:**Prevention (in place):** `client_encoding: 'UTF8'` added to `dialectOptions` in both `designflow-backend/models/db.js` and `designflow-data-syncing/models/db.js`. This forces UTF-8 on the connection so the app can't introduce new mojibake; it does **not** retro-fix existing data.

file.content:
- test allowed and denied cases
- avoid broad permissive policies
- document assumptions
- prefer read-only policies first

## Migrations

Do not rely only on `sequelize.sync()` for production schema evolution.

Current services may call `sequelize.sync()`, but schema changes should be explicit and reviewed.

For Supabase/shared DB work:

- use migration files
- keep migrations idempotent where practical
- avoid destructive operations unless explicitly approved
- separate schema changes from data migration when possible
- document rollback or recovery approach

For service-specific additive changes, existing idempotent startup migrations may remain, but do not add risky destructive startup DDL.

## Testing Requirements

When changing DB connection behavior, test at least:

- service startup
- DB authentication
- schema selection via `SCHEMA`
- one read query
- one write query, if safe
- one important workflow for that service
- idle connection behavior if using Supabase pooler
- BFF timeout behavior for slow/failing DB connections

Suggested service smoke tests:

`designflow-backend`

- login/auth lookup
- current user profile
- RFQ list/detail
- customers
- admin/app settings
- notifications

`designflow-item-master`

- item library list
- item detail
- art piece flows
- file/image related flows if touched

`designflow-tracking`

- production tracking list
- sample tracking list
- licensing tracking
- first request after idle gap

`designflow-data-syncing`

- health endpoint
- API key protection
- dry-run or safe sync operation
- write path only in sandbox

## Things Not To Do

Do not:

- replace Angular with Supabase frontend patterns
- make existing Angular screens talk directly to raw Supabase tables
- remove backend business logic
- introduce a new ŌĆ£Supabase connector serviceŌĆØ between backend and DB unless explicitly approved
- hardcode Supabase credentials
- expose service-role keys to browser code
- use broad RLS policies without review
- point production to Supabase before sandbox validation
- change unrelated app behavior during DB migration
- modify infrastructure or unrelated repos unless the task asks for it

## Best Default Implementation

For most migration tasks, the best default is:

1. Keep existing API behavior unchanged.
2. Keep Sequelize.
3. Change DB config to support Supabase.
4. Use Supabase-safe pooling/SSL.
5. Keep backend business logic intact.
6. Validate in sandbox.
7. Document any service-specific gaps.

## Decision Principle

If the change is about existing Designflow functionality, implement it in the existing backend service.

If the change is about shared schema, cross-app views, RPCs, RLS, or database contracts, implement it in the shared DB migration area.

If unsure, preserve the existing frontend -> BFF -> backend -> Sequelize path and ask before introducing direct Supabase API access.

content.text:
I pulled all three repos and read the canonical DB guidance (`SUPABASE-MIGRATION.md`, the new CI workflow, backend `AGENTS.md ┬¦6/┬¦14`, and the shared-db schema map). Here's the verdict.

## The proper way to make DB changes (current policy)

There are now **two lanes**, split by what kind of change it is (`SUPABASE-MIGRATION.md`, Decision Principle):

1. **Shared/cross-app schema** ŌĆö shared views, RPCs, RLS policies, cross-application data contracts, or `supabase/migrations/*.sql` ŌåÆ **must** live in the canonical `u2giants/shared-db` repo. The mirrored `shared-db/` folder inside each app repo is **read-only**. The new `forbid-shared-db-bypass.yml` CI enforces this: it fails any app-repo PR that adds a `supabase/migrations/*.sql` file or references DB credentials / `supabase db push` in `scripts/`, `.github/workflows/`, or `package.json`.

2. **Service-specific additive changes to an app-owned table** ŌĆö stay in the backend service as an **idempotent `ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS` startup migration in `models/db.js`** + model field + `tests/unit/db.migration.test.js` assertion.

## What that means for my change ŌĆö it's compliant, no rework needed

My change adds `column_group_state` to **`GridViewState`**, which the schema map classifies as **PLM app-owned**, for **existing Designflow RFQ functionality**. That lands squarely in lane 2:

- `SUPABASE-MIGRATION.md` line 264: *"For service-specific additive changes, existing idempotent startup migrations may remain, but do not add risky destructive startup DDL."* ŌĆö mine is idempotent, additive, non-destructive. Ō£ō
- Decision Principle line 340: *"If the change is about existing Designflow functionality, implement it in the existing backend service."* Ō£ō (It's not a shared view/RPC/RLS/contract, so lane 1 doesn't apply.)
- Backend `AGENTS.md ┬¦14`: *"Migrations live in `models/db.js`, not a migrations folder"* ŌĆö the exact pattern I followed.

**And I verified the new CI won't fail the backend PR:** I touched `models/db.js` (not a scanned path), added **no** `supabase/migrations/*.sql`, and your `scripts/backfill-rfq-quote-dates.js` (which *is* in a scanned path) contains none of the forbidden DB-secret/`supabase db push`/`schema_migrations` patterns. I grepped it ŌĆö clean.

## The one honest caveat

Because it's an app-owned additive column, it lives only in the backend service's startup migration ŌĆö the canonical shared-db schema record in `u2giants/shared-db` won't show this column. The doc explicitly **accepts** that tradeoff for service-specific additive changes, so it's allowed. If your team would rather the canonical schema always reflect every column (including app-owned ones), the follow-up would be to also add a mirror migration in the `u2giants/shared-db` repo ŌĆö but that's a separate, optional step, not something the current rules require.

Bottom line: the way I made the DB change matches the sanctioned pattern, and it won't trip the new guardrail. Want me to also mirror the column into `u2giants/shared-db` for schema-record completeness, or leave it as the compliant service-level migration?

content.text:
Sorry ŌĆö let me drop the jargon.

**The situation in plain terms:**

Your database change (the new "column_group_state" slot that remembers which price groups a user collapsed) is done and working. It's allowed and it's in the right place.

There are **two places** a database change *can* be written down:

1. **The app itself** (the backend) ŌĆö where I put it. This is what actually creates the column in the real database. Ō£ģ Done.
2. **A separate "master blueprint" repo** your team keeps, called `shared-db` ŌĆö a central document that lists the whole database's structure in one place, mostly for changes that are shared across *several* apps.

My change only needed #1, and the rules specifically say small additions like this are fine to keep in #1 only. So it's complete and correct.

**My question was just:** do you also want me to add a matching note in that #2 "master blueprint" ŌĆö so the central document is 100% complete and mentions this new column too ŌĆö or leave it as-is?

- **Leave it as-is** (my recommendation): fully working, fully allowed, nothing more to do. The only downside is the central blueprint doesn't list this one small app-specific column.
- **Also mirror it to shared-db**: extra tidiness so the blueprint is exhaustive, but it's optional and touches a different repo.

There's no wrong answer ŌĆö it's purely "do you want the extra bookkeeping, yes or no?" If you're not sure, I'd just leave it; it's done and compliant.

38	
39	## 2. Why `shared-db` is the dangerous one
40	
41	Every app reads and writes the **same tables in the same Supabase project**. A
42	single schema change here can break an app that a different session built months
43	ago. The database has no "just this app" ŌĆö it is always shared. That is why the
44	four rules below are non-negotiable for any database change.
45	
46	## 3. The four anti-collision rules (shared database)
47	
48	1. **One schema change in flight at a time.** Before starting database work,
49	   check whether another change is already in progress (┬¦5). If so, finish or
50	   land that one first, or coordinate with the owner. Two simultaneous schema
51	   edits are the number-one cause of a broken shared database.
52	2. **Preview database first. Production never receives untested schema.** Apply
53	   every migration to the preview branch (`xjcyeuvzkhtzsheknaiu`), prove it
54	   works, *then* promote to production (`qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai`).
55	3. **Additive by default (expand, then contract).** Adding a column or table
56	   cannot break another app. **Renaming or dropping** one that another app reads
57	   *will*. Default to additive changes. Only rename/drop after explicit owner
58	   sign-off and a checked deprecation across all four apps.
59	4. **New timestamped migration files only.** Each change is a new
60	   `YYYYMMDDHHMMSS_*.sql` file. Never edit a migration that has already been
61	   applied anywhere ŌĆö that is how two sessions silently clobber each other.

64	
65	Merge a `shared-db` PR **only when every item is true**:
66	
67	1. `scripts/check-sql.sh` passes.
68	2. `supabase db push --dry-run` against the preview branch is clean (only the
69	   intended changes, no surprise drops/renames).

76	promote to **production only in an approved window**. Docs-only PRs (no schema

97	rather than two competing edits. If they genuinely conflict, explain the trade-off
98	to the owner in plain English and let them choose order.

102	```text

104	Production project ref: qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai

110	
111	- App rewrite guides: [`docs/ai-session-instructions/`](docs/ai-session-instructions/README.md)
112	- Shared branch workflow: [`docs/ai-session-instructions/shared-supabase-branch-workflow.md`](docs/ai-session-instructions/shared-supabase-branch-workflow.md)
113	- Schema ownership map: [`docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md`](docs/unified-supabase-schema-map.md)
114	- Migration risks: [`docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md`](docs/unified-supabase-migration-gaps.md)
115	- CRM production cutover (migrations promoted, Azure OAuth, auto-provision, data import): [`docs/app-migration-notes/popcrm-web-production-cutover-20260621.md`](docs/app-migration-notes/popcrm-web-production-cutover-20260621.md)
116	
117	## 9. Hosted-Supabase gotchas (do not relearn these the hard way)
118	
119	These bit the CRM production cutover (2026-06-21). PM/PIM will hit the same ones.
120	
121	- **PostgREST schema exposure is control-plane config, NOT SQL.** The
122	  `alter role authenticator set pgrst.db_schemas = ...` + `notify pgrst,'reload config'`
123	  statements in `20260621151419_crm_rls_realtime.sql` do **not** take effect on
124	  hosted Supabase ŌĆö the platform overrides them. To expose non-default schemas
125	  (`api, crm, pim, core`) you must call the Management API:
126	  `PATCH https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/{ref}/postgrest`
127	  with `{"db_schema":"public,graphql_public,api,crm,pim,core"}`. It is a per-project
128	  setting; **re-confirm it after any project restore/clone**, and set it on the
129	  preview branch too. If supabase-js suddenly 404s on `api.*`/`crm.*`, check this first.
130	- **`service_role` has no rights on non-`public` schemas by default.** Server-side
131	  scripts/workers using the service-role key get "permission denied for schema core/crm"
132	  until granted. The grants live in `20260621164759_service_role_grants.sql`
133	  (usage + ALL on tables/sequences for `app, core, crm, pim, plm, ingest, api`,
134	  plus default privileges). Re-run/verify after adding new schemas.
135	- **Supabase Auth has one `site_url`; every app needs explicit redirect allowlist entries.**
136	  The production project's Auth `site_url` is `https://crm.designflow.app`, so OAuth
137	  flows for PM/DAM/SG/master-data apps must pass an explicit app-origin `redirectTo`
138	  and that origin must be in `uri_allow_list`. Keep bare origin, trailing-slash origin,
139	  and `/**` wildcard entries for `crm`, `crm-dev`, `pm`, `pm-dev`, `pm-ci`, `dam`,
140	  `sg`, and `master` designflow hosts. If Microsoft SSO from one app lands on CRM,
141	  check `GET /v1/projects/qsllyeztdwjgirsysgai/config/auth` before changing frontend
142	  routing.
143	- **Ingested domains are never customers.** `crm.ingested_domain` is CRM-private
144	  email triage data only. It must not FK to, promote into, source-ref, join as,
145	  feed picker lists for, or otherwise associate with `core.customer`. The
146	  corrective migration `20260629034500_remove_ingested_domain_customer_association.sql`
147	  removed `api.customer_list`, `crm.promote_ingested_domain(...)`,
148	  `crm.ingested_domain.promoted_customer_id`, and all `directus/ingested_domains`
149	  customer source refs after 3,741 polluted refs were found in production.
150	- **`unique nulls not distinct (external_source, external_id)`** on `crm.*` and
151	  `core.*` tables means you cannot bulk-insert many rows with both columns NULL ŌĆö
152	  the second NULL/NULL row collides. Importers must set a real
153	  `external_source` (e.g. `'directus'`) and a unique `external_id` per row.
154	- **Cross-schema FKs (e.g. `crm.department.company_id ŌåÆ core.company`) are real and
